title: SEGMENT 17 — Going the other direction: optogenetics

lines.text: SEGMENT 17 — Going the other direction: optogenetics
I want to spend a segment on a closely related technology that came out of the same broader revolution.

It's not strictly about fluorescent proteins, but it shares all the same ancestry — a marine organism, a light-sensitive protein, clever engineering — and it's increasingly used in combination with fluorescent imaging.
It's called optogenetics, and the central idea is the inverse of fluorescent imaging.
In a fluorescent protein, light goes in and light comes out, and we observe.
In optogenetics, light goes in and the protein does something — opens an ion channel, drives a pump, triggers a signaling event — and we control.
Fluorescent proteins let us watch.
Optogenetic proteins let us drive.
Here's the analogy.
Imagine that channelrhodopsin is a window shade attached to an ion channel.
Shine blue light on it, the shade rolls up, the channel opens, ions flow through.
Shine no light, the shade stays down, the channel is closed.
A neuron expressing channelrhodopsin can be made to fire on command, just by turning on a blue light.

The story begins with a class of proteins called opsins, which are seven-transmembrane proteins that bind the small molecule retinal — the same molecule that allows your own eyes to detect light — and respond to light.
Mammalian opsins, like rhodopsin in your retina, signal slowly through G-protein cascades.
But there's a completely different family — microbial opsins — found in archaea, bacteria, and algae, that work much faster.
They are themselves light-gated ion channels or light-driven ion pumps.
Shine light on them, and ions move across the membrane on a millisecond timescale.

The breakthrough that launched optogenetics came in 2005, when Karl Deisseroth, Ed Boyden, and Georg Nagel showed that you could express channelrhodopsin-2 — a light-gated cation channel from a green alga — in mammalian neurons.
Shine blue light on a ChR2-expressing neuron, and sodium and other cations rush in, the cell depolarizes, an action potential fires.
Suddenly, with no electrode, with no chemicals, just a blue LED, you can make specific neurons fire whenever you want.
That capability is staggering, because for the first time you could causally probe neural circuits.
Not just record what neurons are doing, but make specific neurons fire and ask what behavior follows.
Cell types defined genetically by their promoters could be activated selectively.
The technique exploded across neuroscience over the next decade and is now standard in essentially every behavioral neuroscience lab.
A complementary protein, halorhodopsin, is a yellow-light-driven chloride pump that lets you silence neurons by hyperpolarizing them.
Between channelrhodopsin and halorhodopsin you have complementary on/off switches for neural activity, controllable by different colors of light.
The interaction with fluorescent imaging is where things get really exciting, and it's called all-optical electrophysiology.
The idea: combine an optogenetic actuator — a channelrhodopsin — with a fluorescent indicator — a GCaMP or a voltage sensor — in the same neuron.
Then you can both stimulate neural activity with one wavelength of light, and read out activity with another wavelength, all without any electrodes.
You can drive a single neuron to fire and watch the calcium response in real time.
You can stimulate one population of neurons and image the response in a downstream network.
You can do classical electrophysiology, but on hundreds or thousands of cells at once, in any cell type you can target genetically.
The big challenge in combining these tools is spectral crosstalk.
You don't want your imaging light to accidentally activate your opsin, or vice versa.
So a lot of engineering has gone into separating the excitation spectra cleanly.
Pairings like a red-shifted opsin with a green calcium indicator, or a blue opsin with a red indicator, are now standard.
Why does this matter beyond neuroscience?
Because the principle generalizes.
There are now optogenetic tools to drive protein localization, to dimerize proteins on command, to trigger signaling pathways, to switch gene expression on and off — all with light.
Combined with fluorescent imaging, you can perturb a system and watch it respond in real time, in living cells.

That capability — to drive and to read biology with light — is one of the major themes of modern molecular cell biology, and it grew out of the same insight that drove fluorescent protein development: light is a remarkably clean, fast, spatially-targeted way to interact with biology, if you have the right molecular interfaces.
The lesson for our purposes is that the fluorescent protein toolkit doesn't sit alone.
It's part of a broader optical-control-of-biology toolkit, and the most powerful modern experiments use both halves.
Fluorescent proteins to watch.
Opsins to drive.
Both deployed through the same microscope.
The optogenetic revolution is a sibling of the fluorescent-protein revolution, born of similar curiosity about marine proteins, engineered by similar people, and deployed in the same kinds of labs.
